TextENGNew: ``...The one who went around cutting all the phone cords was you, wasn't it, Rika-chan?@k@vS05/13/101300622. You shouldn't do that anymore.``
``...I have no idea what you're talking about.``
``Really...?``
The girl tried to play dumb, but no expression could have been a more eloquent admission.
It was obvious she was trying to laugh it off, but that adorable smile of hers made me want to just forgive her.
...Ahh... I'm going to make a terrible father someday.
That momentary lapse in my tension was snapped taut again by the girl's next words.
"It's good that the grandson... was found safe."
I couldn't find anything to say in response. I fell silent.
...I already knew this from Sato-san's intel, didn't I.
This girl... no—this entire village had known what I'd come here to do from the very beginning.
I considered playing dumb.
But I decided not to.
With everything already exposed, there was no point in feigning ignorance now...
"...Somehow."
"That's good.@k@vS05/27/100500142. ...Now you can go back to Tokyo."
"...Thank you."
"...Is your injury bad, Akasaka?"
"Hard to say... I haven't heard from the doctor yet, so I don't know how bad it is."
Huh?@k Come to think of it, I vaguely recalled being told to lie down and stay still for at least twenty-four hours.
...Didn't that basically mean complete bed rest...?
"...Injured people shouldn't be sneaking out of hospitals."
"And people shouldn't be cutting phone cords."
"...Mii.@k@vS05/27/100500146. I have no idea what you're talking about."
"Then I don't know what you're talking about either."
After playing dumb at each other, we both broke into laughter at the same time.
The sight of us laughing so merrily together—anyone who saw it would probably have found it quite heartwarming.
"...The odds of Akasaka leaving this village alive were not high at all."
"...I don't want Akasaka to die, so I'm glad you can leave this village alive."
"Rika-chan.@k@vS05/13/101300631. ...You knew who I really was... from the very beginning, didn't you?"
The girl said nothing, but gave a firm, unmistakable nod.
"...Then, I think you understand, but... we're probably something like enemies to this village.@k@vS05/13/101300633. And yet you gave me all those warnings."
That's right.@k She said it back then, too.
She wouldn't warn someone she was fine with seeing die.@k ...She had definitely said something like that.
"Then... I should probably be grateful to you."
"...You should be grateful.@k@vS05/27/100500150. I'm your lifesaver."
With that, she puffed out her chest proudly.
She said it jokingly, but... the meaning lurking beneath those words was heavy.
My fate—whether I lived or died—may have been decided somewhere entirely beyond my knowledge.
She had said it with such an adorable smile that it took me a few moments of silence before the weight of her words truly sank in.
...I had been here before.
It was the stone steps leading up to the Furude Shrine grounds—where the Guardians' office was located.
When I'd passed through earlier, I was so focused on finding a phone booth that I hadn't noticed.
"If I recall correctly... this shrine is your home, right, Rika-chan?"
"...More or less."
Looking up toward the shrine grounds, I could see bright lights and hear the lively sound of voices.
What was going on up there...?
"...Today is a festival day."
"A festival?"
"...Hinamizawa's one and greatest festival.@k@vS05/27/100500154. ...The Watanagashi festival."
"Wata... nagashi?"
"...It's a boring little affair, really—festival in name only.@k@vS05/27/100500156. ...Would you like to see it?"
The girl said it matter-of-factly.
...But she must have seen that I looked interested, because she climbed a couple of steps and beckoned me to follow.
...I'd only be going back to sleep anyway.
If I had nothing else to do, tagging along on the girl's little detour didn't seem like a bad idea.
...This village was enemy territory, and if I strayed from Ooishi down some side path, there was no telling what might happen.
...And yet, being with this girl made me forget all of that.
...For no reason I could explain, I felt that as long as I was with her, the village's hostility would not touch me.
That was why, even knowing my cover was blown, I felt strangely no fear about setting foot in the heart of the Onigafuchi Guardians' territory once more...

Climbing up to the shrine grounds,@k I found about three tents set up, with folding tables and chairs scattered about.@k The village elders—or rather, the Guardians' executives?—were deep into a lively, thoroughly drunken drinking session.
The scene looked like nothing more than a neighborhood association having drinks while enjoying the evening cool.
At the very least, it bore no resemblance to any festival I'd ever seen.
"...See?@k@vS05/27/100500158. Boring festival, isn't it?"
"Haha... this certainly doesn't look like a festival."
"...It's a festival that's been going on since ancient times, but... as you can see, it's fallen into decline."
The girl said that with an exasperated expression, watching the old men carry on.
She was right—this was no festival anymore.
It was just the neighbors gathering to drink, using the festival as a convenient excuse.
"Haha... the deity being honored must feel rather sorry with this turnout..."

"...This pathetic little festival will, in five or six years, have the whole village turning out in force...@k@vS05/27/100500161. and the ceremony will be performed properly. It'll become a proper festival again."
The way she said it was less hopeful speculation... and more like she was announcing a future already set in stone.
Not even a prediction—more like simply reporting an outcome.@k That was the tone of it.
Completely baseless, yet stated in a way that brooked no argument.
It was the same way she spoke when she 'changed.'
While I struggled to find words, the girl walked off toward the far side of the grounds without any concern for me.
I couldn't afford to be left behind, so I hurried after her.
Passing through a grove of pine trees, a strong, cool gust of wind scattered my bangs.
...It was the same lookout point with the splendid view that the girl had shown me that evening.
With the sky now fully dark, the modest lights dotting the landscape offered a view quite different from daytime.
"...Looking at it like this, the village seems quite peaceful."
The girl said it with an indifferent expression.
"The other day,@k@vS05/13/101300641. ...you stated with certainty that this village would never be sunk beneath a dam."
The girl didn't answer, seemingly surrendering herself to the sensation of the wind teasing through her hair.
"That meant... even at that point, the Minister had already given in to the demands, didn't it?"
The girl spun around and met my gaze.
But she said nothing.
She offered not a single word that would confirm or deny it.
"This case... will probably be buried in darkness.@k@vS05/13/101300644. ...Headquarters probably doesn't want the trouble of the Minister's scandal going public."
"...They must have threatened to kill the grandson otherwise."
"And when the negotiations with the Minister succeeded... the grandson was released."
"...It was you who saved the grandson, Akasaka."
"I wonder about that.@k@vS05/13/101300647. ...The discovery of that wallet that started everything...@k@vS05/13/101300648. even that felt almost too convenient."
...Ever since that wallet was found, I'd been carrying a nagging sense that something was off.
Ooishi had said it too.
That the order to release the hostage had been given from the Sonozaki main house.
In other words... our dramatic manhunt had been a staged farce from the very beginning.
...Come to think of it, I'd been punched, kicked, and had a gun pointed at me.
It felt like they put up quite a fight, but... even that may have been nothing more than a well-crafted performance.
Once the Minister complied with the negotiations, they couldn't very well just say "here you go" and hand the grandson back at the front door.
...Because they knew Public Safety had identified it as a kidnapping and was already in motion.
So the story would be that the grandson was rescued safely thanks to Public Safety's actions.
And therefore the Minister had not yielded to any unjust demands.@k ...That kind of staging was necessary.
"...I don't understand these complicated things."
"...Same here.@k@vS05/13/101300650. Even for me, the complicated parts are hard to untangle.@k@vS05/13/101300651. But there's one thing I'm certain of."
"It's exactly as you said.@k@vS05/13/101300653. ...This village won't sink beneath any dam.@k@vS05/13/101300654. The dam project will be scrapped before long."
That wasn't something a police officer should be saying.
But the words came out naturally.
This village may indeed have been a dangerous place.
But that was because they were protecting their homeland.
If someone unilaterally told me to leave the home I'd grown accustomed to, I would surely be furious too.
And unlike me, moving from rental to rental to follow my job, this was land their ancestors had lived on for generations.
...It might be presumptuous to call their desperate resistance excessive.
Maybe I had no right to weigh in on politics or governance.
...But deliberately choosing a village where people loved their land this deeply as the site for a dam—that was something I simply couldn't agree with.
"Still, isn't that a good thing?"
"...How so?"
"At the very least... this means peace will return to the village."
Even if the Minister had promised to push for the dam plan's cancellation with all his influence, it wouldn't be reversed within a few short months.
At the very least... yes, it might take a year or two.
It would be some time yet before the villagers officially heard that their village would not be sunk at the bottom of a dam.
But... the promise that peace would return to the village had undeniably been made.
In the end, from start to finish... everything had already been decided.
Just as the girl had said, it was all decided.
There had been no need for me to come here in the first place.
No need to come here and stir up trouble in an already volatile situation.
If I had never come here—
The grandson's release might have been carried out far more peacefully.
"...Peace?@k@vS05/27/100500170. In this village?"
I nodded and smiled at her reassuringly.
changeReason: Old says 'she laughed as if to admonish me' but JPN 私は...少女に諭すように笑いかけた means 'I smiled at the girl reassuringly' — reversed subject of the action.

TextENGNew: But... the girl's expression remained flat, utterly blank.
And then... her eyes narrowed slightly, and she let out a soft, stifled laugh.
"...Even though something bloody will happen every year from now on?@k@vS05/27/100500172. ...Heeheeheeheehee."
"Rika-chan... what are you talking about?"
The wind scattering her hair, the girl gazed at my face with the pleasure of someone savoring knowledge she alone possessed...@k and continued to giggle softly for a while.
"You know...@k@vS05/27/100500174. ...In a few more years... I'm going to be killed."
Huh...?
Silhouetted against the moon, her face cast in shadow by the backlighting, it was hard to tell whether her expression was a smile, amusement...@k or resignation.
"...It's a very unpleasant thing, but...@k@vS05/27/100500176. that, too, has probably already been decided."
"Decided? Who would decide something like that?!"
``I'd like to know that as well.``
Turning back to face me, she showed me a resolute expression for the first time...
And after staring into my eyes for a moment, she turned back to gaze at the scenery before she spoke her next words.
``...There's a lot of people who have no regard for human life.@k@vS05/27/100500179. ...Even if I tell you this, it might not change anything, though.
But... if you want to erase the specter of death, sometimes, like with the reflection of the moon on the water, all you have to do is throw a small stone.``
I had no idea what she was trying to say.
It's just... I had to listen to every word with my body and soul, or at least, that's what I thought.
``...This day next year.@k@vS05/27/100500182. ...Yes, June 1979, on this day.@k@vS05/27/100500183. The foreman of the dam construction project will be killed.``
For a moment, I couldn't comprehend what she was talking about.
By the foreman... did she mean that gruff old man I played mahjong with?
``...B-Be killed... ...What makes you think...``
She didn't answer.
But she had the same expression on her face as she did when she declared that the dam project would go away.
...That wasn't the face of somebody making a forecast or a prediction.
...It was something that was already decided-- no, it was as if she was already reporting the results... that sort of expression.
``...After being killed in a gruesome manner, the body will be torn apart and disposed of.``
``...Murdered and dismembered...``
``...The next year, in June of 1980 on this day.@k@vS05/27/100500186. ...Satoko's parents will be pushed to their deaths.``
...Satoko?
It was the first time I heard that name, but I didn't have the courage to interrupt the girl's story.
``...It might be an accident.@k@vS05/27/100500188. ...An unfortunate accident.``
The girl smiled faintly... but she wasn't exactly encouraging me to smile along with the lifeless look in her eyes.
``And then, the year after that, this day in June of 1981.@k@vS05/27/100500190. ...My parents will be killed.
And then the year after that, this day in June of 1982.@k@vS05/27/100500192. Satoko's abusive aunt will have her head split open and die.
And then the year after that, this day in the June of 1983...@k@vS05/27/100500194. ...Or possibly a few days afterwards.
...I will be killed.
If all the deaths are pre-ordained...@k@vS05/27/100500197. ...Then the last one would also be all according to plan.@k@vS05/27/100500198. ...But then whose plan would it be...?``
This village is full of murderers and people who have no regard for human life.
It would be fine to believe that all the deaths up to 1982 would be the work of somebody in the village.
It would be fine to believe that all the deaths that would occur were at the discretion of those who control the village.
But that didn't explain what would happen in 1983.
There was no way that the last death would fit in with anybody's plans.
They had no regard for human life.
They would use every means at their disposal to meet their goals.
But their goals were at odds with the last death.
That's why the last death was unrelated to them.
But that last death would definitely,@k most likely,@k probably,@k without question,@k happen.
Probably, definitely, I dare say.
The last death would be a merciful one.
A handkerchief or something being placed over their mouth, making them lose consciousness, never to wake up again.
Exactly whose plan was that?
``...I want to live happily.@k@vS05/27/100500214. ...That's my only wish.
Surrounded by my precious friends, spending our days enjoyably.@k@vS05/27/100500216. ...Only that.@k@vS05/27/100500217. I don't wish for anything more than that.``
``...Rika-chan...``
``...I don't want to die.``
The girl, stoic as ever, let those last words slip in the end.
...Eventually, we were discovered by some drunks.
The girl, as well as I, were dragged back to the festivities.
Everybody was completely drunk, so even when they saw my head completely wrapped in bandages, they wouldn't let me leave.
...Well certainly, if I was in good enough condition to slip out of the hospital, it must not be that serious.
The girl was mingling with everybody, being pampered like a kitten.
Her expression was adorable... nothing like the one from just before,@k predicting her own death and whispering that she didn't want to die.
Having said that...
...Having a silly and lively conversation like this, it was hard to believe that these people were the core of an extremist resistance group.

Even if it was a shell of its former self, it was a festival where people in the village gathered, so the twenty-some-odd folk here should have included some of the village executives and other important members.
...Among those, there was no doubt that there were members that were at the Sonozaki family council in attendance.
Meaning, even if we never met face-to-face before, there were people here who knew my identity.
In a small village like this, they knew the faces of all the villagers that lived here.
That's why, without even saying anything, it soon became obvious that I was an outsider.

But they actually seemed interested by that, asking me all sorts of questions about Tokyo,@k drinking while musing on the faint relationship the city had with the villagers and empathizing with the rather meager environment.
The confidential talks with the Minister were finished, his grandson was in police custody... everything was over.
I might not have to be put in another perilous situation.
To me, they were only my adversaries because of my job.@k Outside of that, there was no reason I couldn't be friendly with them.
...The next time I visited this village...
would it return to being a tranquil and beautiful place?
If that happened, I might be able to take a relaxed walk around the village with her again.
My cup was once again filled to the brim with beer...
By the time I was able to get away, it was the middle of the night.
The girl had at some point disappeared.
It seems that her parents had told her to go home and sleep when she let out a large yawn.
The old folks, who had absolutely no remorse for making an injured person hang out with them, told me as much as they laughed.
I was just there drinking, so I had thought to help a bit with clean up. However, my help was refused on the grounds that I was a guest.
On top of that, they had a car prepared to take me back to the hospital.
Losing the battle against my sleepiness inside the car, I was sound asleep until we reached the parking lot of the hospital.
The front door of the hospital was naturally locked at this hour.
I wandered around bewilderedly searching for a back entrance until somebody on night duty found out and let me inside.
``Where were you this late at night?@k@vS05/00/manC00015. Dr. Irie was worried, you know...``
``...Sorry for the trouble.@k@vS05/13/101300666. I was held up by the people having a festival at the shrine...``
``Oh, they did say something about everybody in the town council getting together for a drink.@k@vS05/00/manC00017. My condolences.@k@vS05/00/manC00018. ...Are your wounds all right?``
``...They're throbbing a bit.@k@vS05/13/101300668. It's probably because of the alcohol, I think.``
``Oh, that's right.@k@vS05/00/manC00020. The detective that was with you wanted to get in touch.``
``...Oops.@k@vS05/13/101300670. I kept Ooishi-san waiting...``
I glanced at the clock.
No matter how you looked at it, it was way too late to call him now.@k I'll apologize to him tomorrow over the phone...
I returned back to my hospital room and slipped into the bed that was still unmade from the time I went outside.
...Oh, that's no good.@k I have to turn off the lights.
......But my weariness had cut the cord powering my body, lulling me into a deep sleep.
